name: Blood Gas Analyzer (Generic)
description: Measures acid-base balance, electrolytes, and blood gases in clinical specimens.
parameters: pCO2 - Partial pressure of carbon dioxide
troubleshooting: Air bubbles in sample: Expel immediately, samples are anaerobic
Delayed analysis: Re-analyze if delay > 15 minutes, apply ice bath if delays unavoidable
Calibration drift: Perform 2-point calibration before critical samples
Out-of-range results: Verify electrode function, check standard solutions
notes: Critical for ICU and emergency departments. Understand acid-base compensation mechanisms for clinical interpretation.

name: Bricyte E6
description: Automated hematology analyzer with Flow Cytometry. Suitable for clinical laboratories and hospital settings.
parameters: WBC - Total White Blood Cell Count
troubleshooting: Cold sample: Warm to room temperature (18-25°C) before analysis
Clotted sample: Discard, recollect in proper anticoagulant (EDTA K2)
Low platelet count: Verify no clumping, review blood smear
Instrument error E001: Check reagent bottles and perform purge cycle
Abnormal WBC differential: Review with microscope for morphology assessment
High NTU (turbidity): Check for hemolysis or lipemia, recollect if necessary
No aspiration: Verify sample level in tube, check sampling needle is clear
notes: Flow Cytometry Analyzer of Mindray. Compatible with EDTA / Heparin anticoagulated whole blood specimens. Results include complete CBC with differential and RBC/platelet indices. Regular maintenance and calibration required for optimal performance.

definition: Interactive tool for reading and interpreting PT and APTT optical reaction curves on the Mindray CX-6000 / CX-6010 coagulation analyzer. Covers Original curve (Luminous Flux), 1. Deriv., and 2. Deriv. across 7 clinical scenarios.
explanation: Turbidimetry-based clot detection: the analyzer uses a tri-color LED (405 nm, 545 nm, 660 nm) to measure luminous flux through the plasma sample. As fibrin polymerizes, flux decreases in a sigmoid pattern. The tool displays and explains all three IFU-defined curves: Original reaction curve (Y-axis = Luminous Flux), 1. Deriv. (rate of change; peak = Clot Point), and 2. Deriv. (acceleration; zero crossing confirms Clot Point). An Index tab defines all curve landmarks: Start Point, Clot Point, End Point, and Amplitude — using official terminology from the CX-6000/CX-6010 Operator Manual (IFU).
clinicalSignificance: Essential for lab staff to recognize normal vs. abnormal curve patterns: prolonged PT (factor VII / warfarin), prolonged APTT (heparin / inhibitor), low amplitude (fibrinogen deficiency), pre-clotted sample (no reaction / ERROR), and biphasic 1. Deriv. (heparin effect). Supports correct action: mixing study, Clauss fibrinogen, sample rejection, INR / APTT ratio interpretation.
